So seminar of defiance, socio-politically, economically. And so it's sort of against that backdrop that here we all are talking about a topic that many would consider, I guess, to be unseemly. The good news, I guess, of it, as I went through questionnaires, is that the majority of you in the, how have you been affected by the recession, for the most part, your businesses are doing well. And so that's good news, you know. Everybody, to one degree or another, seems to be either doing almost as good, or doing as good, or in many cases, doing better. Probably the best report, if anybody in the room, was from Bill Goh, where are you, Bill? You wanna, there you are, yeah. Kicking butt and taking names. Streamlining, tightening a little bit, he indicated, but having a great year. And so I heard that from a lot of people, and that's nice. I imagine, like me, you don't hear that from a lot of people in general. Now, asking the typical business owner, the, yeah, just the courtesy question, how's business, is, you know, like asking a 70-year-old how you're feeling. You know, you get an answer that is in greater detail, and greater depth, and more traumatic than you ever wanted to know, you know. They're dropping their drawers to show you the scars from the latest operation, and the business owners are pretty quick, you know, to do the same thing. So, I think we all come together with kind of a, kind of a different mindset, which is pretty cool. So, here's where we're gonna go. Seven requirements of seven figures. These are the seven categories of things that we are gonna be talking about in our time together. And following that page, I give you a couple of pages you might wanna use to take notes with. So you might wanna rip them out of your book, and you might wanna use those as a framework to take notes. So we're gonna talk about creating and sustaining and using status. Tomorrow we are gonna talk about leverage. And just as a piece of trivia, it reminds me, there's a halfway decent show on TNT if you haven't seen it called leverage, which is actually, it's worth watching if you're a TV watcher, it's actually pretty good. And we're gonna talk about productivity. And then on our last day, we're gonna talk about resistance, and we're gonna talk a little bit about personal philosophy. So that's where we're gonna go. And we begin. So, status. The page that looks like this. Right out of Webster. And the four words I would call your attention to here that are pretty important. Relative and rank. And what you need to know is whether you do it for them or not, people are always taking people that they consider to be in a common group. And they are ranking them. They are thinking about them in relation to others that they know. So hardly anybody is able to operate in a vacuum free of comparison to others, no matter how hard we try. So on a big scale, some nitwits always making a list. So all these celebrities have to put up with wherever I threw it every year, which is why Trump is constantly suing people about this. So these people have to put up with Forbes ranking them. And then making a big deal out of the rankings. And Forbes is even, not only are they ranking them in how much money they make, which think about that for a second. So in your community, how would you like to have published in the newspaper once a year?
